name: Gary Bartz
abstract: Gary Bartz (born September 26, 1940) is an American jazz saxophonist. He has won two Grammy Awards.

concerts.label: JVC Jazz Festival: A Lullaby for Birdland
Carnegie Hall Jazz Band: Jerome Kern – All the Things You Are
concerts.venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage

works.t: Very Warm for May: All the Things You Are

concerts.label: American Music Group: Influences Billy Strayhorn

works.t: A Flower Is A Lovesome Thing
Ballad For Very Tired And Very Sad

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: The group was Gary Bartz on alto saxophone, Billy Harper on tenor saxophone, Kenny Barron, Buster Williams, and Idris Mohammed on drums.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lonnie Liston Smith
about_quotes.evidence: We used to hang out. And we ended up playing together in his father’s club called the North End Lounge
about_quotes.doc_title: Lonnie Liston Smith interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 9, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: He said, “I heard Gary Bartz. Really sounded great.” We talked to Gary. Gary said yeah. So we rehearsed. Same thing. We put our money together and did a record.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Denny Zeitlin
about_quotes.evidence: So I started sitting in there typically on weekends and sometimes even during the week and playing with Gary.
about_quotes.doc_title: Denny Zeitlin interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 8, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Christian McBride
about_quotes.evidence: And on the other half of the album, Gary Bartz is playing alto saxophone
about_quotes.doc_title: Christian McBride interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Blue
about_quotes.evidence: And Gary Bartz was in that band at that time. He was there as well.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Blue interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 23, 2022
